event: UFC Fight Night: Burns vs. Malott

items.fight_news: Malott is headlining a UFC card for the first time, and does so in Canada. The crowd factor at Canada Life Centre is expected to be one of the loudest environments for a Fight Night in recent memory.

Burns is on a 4-fight losing streak, with his last win coming over Jorge Masvidal in 2023. He has since dropped decisions and stoppages to Belal Muhammad, Jack Della Maddalena, Sean Brady, and Michael Morales - all elite-level opposition, but the slide is real.

Burns publicly discussed delayed concussion symptoms following his TKO loss to Michael Morales in May 2025. He has not fought in nearly 11 months heading into this bout, one of the longest inactivity stretch of his recent career.
Malott holds a 3-fight win streak with wins over Kevin Holland, Charles Radtke (KO R2), and Trevin Giles. He has 9 first-round finishes across his career and an overall finish rate above 80%.

Prediction models heavily favor Malott. At 34 vs Burns' 39, the age gap, activity advantage, and momentum all point toward the Canadian, though Burns has repeatedly proven he can compete against top opposition even in losing efforts.

This is a 5-round main event, which benefits Burns stylistically - he is one of the most experienced welterweights in UFC history and his BJJ is elite. If he can stay durable and take the fight deep, the equation shifts.

Jourdain is on a 2-fight win streak since moving down to bantamweight, submitting Davey Grant and Victor Henry via guillotine - both earned Performance of the Night bonuses. He arrives as the co-main event favorite fighting in his home country.
Phillips is on a 2-fight losing streak, dropping decisions to Rob Font and Vinicius Oliveira. His three-fight win streak was snapped by Font in 2024 and he has not looked like the same fighter since.

Jourdain's guillotine has been his signature weapon at 135 - two consecutive finishes with the same submission suggests either an elite setup or opponents walking into it. Phillips' wrestling should change that dynamic compared to Jourdain's recent opponents.

This is Nallo's UFC debut at 36 years old after earning his contract via Dana White's Contender Series in September 2025 with a first-round KO. All 14 of his professional wins have come by stoppage - he has never been to a decision as a winner.

Nallo is a former Bellator fighter who trains at TriStar Gym in Montreal alongside elite competition, and was a former training partner of Georges St-Pierre. His late UFC arrival does not reflect his skill level.

Herbert is 3-5 in the UFC and coming off a split decision loss to Chris Padilla in March 2025. At 37 years old, he is a durable and experienced opponent but has struggled to string together wins at this level.

Silva is a Brazilian submission specialist with 11 career wins by submission. Her finishing ability from bottom position is the primary threat in this fight. She has submitted opponents from positions that looked like control for the other fighter on the scorecards.

Jasudavicius has a meaningful striking advantage on the feet. Her gameplan should be to avoid grappling exchanges and keep Silva at range, where her striking output and footwork give her a consistent scoring edge.

This fight has a clear style split: Jasudavicius wins standing, Silva wins on the ground. Whoever dictates where the fight happens will likely dictate the outcome. Any wrestling exchange is a danger for Jasudavicius regardless of who initiates it, because Silva creates submissions from unexpected positions.

Moises is a veteran Brazilian lightweight with elite BJJ and a track record of finishing UFC-level opponents with triangles and rear-naked chokes, often from bottom position when opponents believe they have control on the ground.

Young is a physical and athletic prospect with a wrestling-first approach. He will look to use top position and ground and pound, which puts him directly in the zone where Moises is most dangerous. The irony is that Young's strengths play into Moises' submission game.

Experience is the defining factor in this matchup. Young has the physical tools to win, but managing pace and positional awareness across a full 15 minutes against a veteran submission artist is a different challenge than anything on his resume so far.

Barbosa earned his UFC contract through the Contender Series in 2025 with sharp hands and aggressive output. His finishing rate is high but his DWCS opponents were a level below what he will face in the Octagon. Whether his power and timing translate against more resilient opposition is the central question.

Buzukja is the more experienced UFC fighter and enters as the measured favorite. His striking volume and consistent output have been his calling card in the UFC, and that style plays well against a Barbosa who likes to engage in open exchanges.

Both fighters are at the stage of their UFC careers where a finish earns a bonus and a decision earns a next fight. Expect aggression from the opening bell. Barbosa's one-punch power is the biggest upset variable if he catches Buzukja in an early exchange.

Leblanc is a Canadian middleweight with a grappling-heavy, forward-pressure style. Valentin is the technical striking favorite who will look to keep the fight at range where his stand-up is more effective than Leblanc's wrestling-first approach.

Limited public footage on both fighters makes this one of the harder prelim bouts to assess going in. The fighter who establishes his preferred range early in round 1 is likely to control the scorecards, as neither man has shown the ability to dramatically shift game plans mid-fight based on available tape.

Leblanc's forward pressure tends to disrupt technical strikers who rely on consistent range management. If he can close distance and make the fight physically uncomfortable in the clinch, his grappling creates genuine problems even for more skilled stand-up fighters.

Boser has competed against legitimate UFC heavyweights throughout his career, including Ciryl Gane, Ilir Latifi, and Andrei Arlovski. Those fights produced losses but also confirmed his durability and striking power are real at this level. Against Saricam, he is the clear experience and skill favorite.

Saricam enters with minimal public footage at the UFC level, which is a genuine scouting problem for any opponent. Unknown heavyweights with strong regional records are among the most dangerous prelim opponents in the sport precisely because there is no established pattern to prepare against.

Boser's tendency to brawl rather than box has historically given opponents more opportunities than the skill gap should allow. A disciplined, structured game plan from his corner matters more in this fight than the talent differential between the two fighters would suggest on paper.

Croden is an aggressive women's bantamweight who builds her game around forward pressure and consistent output. Her style is designed to make fights physically uncomfortable through volume rather than technical precision.

Zheleznyakova brings a striking-first approach with solid technical fundamentals and has faced stiffer competition on paper heading into this fight. Her edge is in technical refinement and composure, not raw output.

The central question is who controls the range. Zheleznyakova wants to box at distance and use footwork to neutralize Croden's pressure. Croden wants to close the gap and make it physical. Whoever imposes their preferred distance in the first two minutes is likely to carry the round and the fight.

Aldrich is a UFC veteran in women's flyweight with years of experience managing pace and accumulating volume over three rounds. Her ability to control the tempo of a fight is the core advantage she brings against a less experienced opponent.

Horth is a Canadian prospect with real physical tools, but her ability to maintain consistent output and adapt mid-fight against a UFC veteran has not yet been proven over three full rounds at this level.

Horth needs to impose aggression early and disrupt Aldrich's rhythm before she settles in. If Aldrich gets comfortable working at range in round 1, her volume and experience compound across rounds 2 and 3 in a way that becomes very difficult for Horth to overcome on the scorecards.

Raposo is a Canadian flyweight with strong finishing instincts and consistent early-fight aggression. He applies pressure from the opening bell and has built his UFC reputation on exciting, finish-oriented performances.

Nascimento is a Brazilian flyweight with a solid technical base and meaningful grappling credentials. His ground game gives him a credible path to neutralizing Raposo's stand-up pressure if he can dictate where the fight goes.

This is a genuinely competitive prelim bout. Raposo's aggression and finishing instincts on the feet against Nascimento's composure and grappling makes the outcome more uncertain than most flyweight prelims on the card.

Castaneda is a durable UFC bantamweight veteran with a long promotional run and consistent fight IQ. His ability to manage pace, absorb pressure, and counter-strike against aggressive opponents is his primary structural advantage in this fight.

Vologdin is a Russian prospect with a strong regional finishing rate. His UFC career is still short and this fight is the most direct test of whether his finishing ability translates against a fighter with Castaneda's timing, resistance, and counter-striking experience.

Castaneda's counter-punching style is specifically effective against aggressive, forward-moving prospects who overcommit on power shots. If Vologdin attacks without discipline and control, Castaneda has the experience to make him pay for it across three rounds.

Siraj makes his UFC debut after earning his contract at BFL with consecutive rear-naked choke finishes. Both submissions came in round 2, pointing to a deliberate tactical pattern: he reads his opponent in round 1 and applies the finish once he has mapped the entry point.

Yannis has been inactive since August 2025 and carries a 2-2-1 professional record. His first two wins came by KO but his career has been inconsistent. Against a submission specialist with a clear finishing template, his ground defense is the most important variable heading in.

Siraj's long regional career in Canada and his debut on home soil in Winnipeg adds context to his preparation. Fighters who earn UFC contracts via submission finishes on regional circuits tend to be composed under pressure rather than erratic. A debut-fight nerves collapse is less likely for a 31-year-old veteran than the spotlight framing suggests.
